summary: The paper introduces DeepAnalyze, an agentic LLM aimed at autonomous, end-to-end data science: taking raw data through analysis all the way to research reports without relying on a predefined workflow. The central methodological contribution is a curriculum-based agentic training paradigm that mirrors how human data scientists learn, progressing from single-ability supervised fine-tuning to multi-ability agentic reinforcement learning in realistic computational environments. To supply training signal for this curriculum, the authors propose a data-grounded trajectory synthesis framework and release the resulting DataScience-Instruct-500K dataset. The trained 8B-parameter model is evaluated across 13 benchmarks spanning tasks from data question answering to open-ended data research, where it is reported to be competitive with, and in several cases to outperform, workflow-based agents built on much larger proprietary LLMs. The system and data are fully open-sourced.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well motivated and addresses a genuinely important problem: moving from brittle, workflow-based data agents toward a trainable agent capable of autonomous, end-to-end data science. The exposition is clear and easy to follow; the introduction lays out the motivation well, and the two-stage curriculum (single-ability supervised fine-tuning followed by multi-ability agentic reinforcement learning) is presented in an accessible way. The empirical evaluation is a clear strength: it spans 13 benchmarks covering a wide range of data tasks, and the headline result of an 8B-parameter model competitive with, and sometimes surpassing, workflow agents built on much larger proprietary LLMs is compelling. The release of the system together with the DataScience-Instruct-500K dataset is a valuable contribution that should benefit the community.

On soundness, my main reservation concerns the attribution of the reported gains. The ablation study is too limited to fully support the design as presented: Table 6 removes only the "Understand" action, leaving the other four actions (Analyze, Code, Execute, Answer) unexamined, so the independent contribution of each action, and whether functional redundancy exists among them, cannot be assessed. The paper motivates the five-action space by analogy to human data scientists but offers no systematic argument that this particular set is necessary and sufficient. A more informative ablation would compare DeepAnalyze against its own base model (DeepSeek-R1-0528-Qwen3-8B) to isolate the value of the special action tokens and the curriculum, i.e., whether structured actions genuinely outperform unstructured reasoning. This is underscored by Table 4, where the base model outperforms DeepAnalyze on some subtasks. Several important hyperparameters are introduced without any sensitivity analysis, and report quality on DABStep-Research is judged exclusively by an LLM, with no human evaluation to anchor the results. I would also encourage more careful language: the claim of "unprecedented performance across 13 benchmarks" overstates what is, on close reading, a mixed picture. Finally, because benchmarks in this area are frequently used both to develop and to evaluate systems, the manuscript should clarify the extent to which the evaluation set is genuinely held out.

On significance and originality, the hybrid framing that combines a predefined action set with post-training reinforcement learning to navigate the trade-off between rigid workflows and free-form prompting is a sensible and timely contribution to an important area, and the curriculum-based agentic training paradigm is a fresh angle. That said, the significance is currently limited by the difficulty of extracting generalizable knowledge from the work: because the contributions of the individual components are not disentangled, it is hard to know which design decisions drive the results or whether the lessons transfer to systems built with different choices. Strengthening the ablations would materially increase both the significance and the originality of the contribution.

On presentation, the paper is generally clear and well structured. It could be improved by adding qualitative examples alongside the quantitative results, both cases where DeepAnalyze succeeds while prior systems fail and its characteristic failure modes (the appendix examples are lengthy and do not call out failure modes or key differences). The clarity of Figure 4 could be improved, and the related-work discussion would benefit from broader and more recent coverage.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. Were any of the evaluation benchmarks used at any point to develop, tune, or select the system, or is the reported evaluation fully held out? If they were used for development, please indicate which ones and how many evaluation-feedback cycles were involved.
2. What justifies the specific five-action space (Analyze, Understand, Code, Execute, Answer) as necessary and sufficient? Can you provide ablations that isolate the contribution of each action, and that compare the special action tokens against the base LLM's unstructured reasoning to quantify their added value?
3. Can you provide a sensitivity analysis for the key hyperparameters introduced during curriculum training?
4. The report-quality evaluation on DABStep-Research relies solely on LLM-as-a-judge. Can you corroborate these results with human evaluation, or at least report inter-judge agreement and robustness to the choice of judge?
5. Can you add qualitative examples that highlight cases where DeepAnalyze succeeds while prior systems fail, together with its characteristic failure modes?
limitations: The limitations discussion is currently thin. I would encourage the authors to add a critical treatment of the benchmarks themselves, including their design limitations and the risk of overfitting when the same benchmarks inform both development and evaluation, and to discuss computational cost and scalability as well as the system's dependence on the specific tool and environment stack.
